publications.title: Allelic polymorphism in the T cell receptor and its impact on immune responses.
publications.authors: Gras S, Chen Z, Miles JJ, Liu YC, Bell MJ, Sullivan LC, Kjer-Nielsen L, Brennan RM, Burrows JM, Neller MA, Khanna R, Purcell AW, Brooks AG, McCluskey J, Rossjohn J, Burrows SR.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined the EBV-specific T cell response restricted by HLA-B*3501 to the HPVGEADYFEY peptide, comparing the commonly selected TRBV9*01 TCR variant (TK3) with the natural allelic variant TRBV9*02, which differs by a single amino acid (Gln55His) near the CDR2β loop. Crystal structures of both TCR variants in complex with HLA-B*3501–peptide revealed that the His55 substitution in TRBV9*02 disrupts charge complementarity at the TCR–pMHC interface, impairing recognition of the cognate EBV epitope and explaining why TRBV9*02 is rarely or never selected during this immune response. This finding demonstrates that genetic polymorphism within TCR loci—rather than just HLA variation—can functionally constrain T cell repertoire selection and influence infection outcomes, with implications for understanding variable responsiveness to pathogens and potential links to autoimmunity-associated TCR alleles.

publications.title: A molecular basis for the interplay between T cells, viral mutants, and human leukocyte antigen micropolymorphism.
publications.authors: Liu YC, Chen Z, Neller MA, Miles JJ, Purcell AW, McCluskey J, Burrows SR, Rossjohn J, Gras S.
publications.executive_summary: This study examined how a single HLA-B*35 polymorphism at two closely related alleles (HLA-B*35:01 and HLA-B*35:08) differentially presents an Epstein-Barr virus epitope (HPVGEADYFEY) and its naturally occurring variants, revealing the molecular basis for altered T cell receptor recognition. Mutations within the epitope at positions 4 and 5 differentially affected peptide flexibility and conformation when bound to each HLA variant, demonstrating how viral escape variants can shift their MHC restriction between nearly identical alleles. A single P5 substitution (Glu to Asp) remarkably reversed restriction preference from HLA-B*35:01 to HLA-B*35:08, illustrating how minimal viral mutations can exploit HLA micropolymorphism. This work explains the evolutionary interplay between HLA genetic diversity, TCR repertoire diversity, and viral mutation, showing how population-level HLA polymorphism constrains viral escape by ensuring that escape variants affecting one allele remain vulnerable to T cell recognition when presented on alternative HLA alleles.
